figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of the sTM network architecture. Graphical description of network according to [39]. Populations: Ek: excitatory populations. Ik: inhibitory populations. Xk: bottom-up inputs (k = 1, . . . ,M). T : top-down input (E1 hosts the assembly representing the first element of the input sequence). Connectivity rules: Triangular arrow heads: excitatory connections. Circular arrow heads: inhibitory connections. Solid lines: static connections. Dashed lines: plastic connections. Kin: random connectivity with fixed indegree. Kout: random connectivity with fixed outdegree. Ω: all-to-all connectivity. ¬M: multiple connections between two neurons not permitted. ¬A: self-connections not permitted. Recurrent connections between excitatory neurons (dashed) target distal dendritic branches and may trigger dendritic action potentials (dAPs) signaling predictions. Connectivity parameters: synaptic weights Jxy, spike transmission delays dxy (x, y ∈ {E, I,X,T}). See “Methods” for detailed mathematical description.

Figure 2: Encoding long intervals and rhythm through repetitive elements. Network activity (middle row) after learning a melody (top row) presented at baseline speed (A) and a third of the baseline speed (B). Gray musical notes in the top row of panel B indicate repeated presentations of the preceding note, each with identical duration. Red dots in the middle row mark spikes, blue lines indicate dAP plateaus. The bottom row shows the readout neuron activity (violet: inactive, yellow: active), obtained by decoding the assembly identity from the spiking activity.

Figure 3: Illustration of the effect of different types of background activity on the membrane potential of an excitatory neuron in the sTM model. The top row shows the external background current Iext applied to the neuron: a constant input of magnitude Ī = 100 pA (A) or an oscillatory input with frequency f = 50Hz, amplitude a = 150 pA, and offset Ī = 50 pA (B). In both cases, the neuron additionally receives a predictive input to its dendrite at around 40ms (middle row). The resulting dendritic current Idend increases until it reaches a threshold IdAP (gray dashed line), triggering a plateau potential IdAP. The superposition of background and dendritic input leads to a strong depolarization of the somatic membrane potential V (bottom row) and a crossing of the spike threshold Vθ (gray dashed line). The neuron elicits a somatic spike (red star), which, in turn, triggers a reset of the dendritic current.

Figure 5: Modulation of replay speed by oscillatory background input. A: Dependence of the replay speed fout on the relative oscillation amplitude a/IdAP and the oscillation frequency f during replay of the reference melody (Fig. 2A). The oscillation offset Ī is zero. The replay speed is averaged across eight different oscillation phases. The gray arrow in the colorbar marks the encoding speed fenc = 1/∆T = 25Hz. Hatched areas represent parameter regions in which stable replay occurs for fewer than four of the eight phases. B: Same as A, but for relative replay speed fout/f . Green and blue bands correspond to regions where the replay speed is an integer fraction of the oscillation frequency, i.e., fout = f , f/2, f/3, etc. Yellow regions indicate replay speeds exceeding the oscillation frequency, fout > f . Bold letters in A and B mark the parameter settings illustrated in panels C–H. C–H: background oscillation (top) and corresponding replay activity (bottom) for different oscillation frequencies f and amplitudes a: f = 18Hz, a/IdAP = 1.1 (C), f = 25Hz, a/IdAP = 1.1 (D), f = 32Hz, a/IdAP = 1.1 (E), f = 50Hz, a/IdAP = 1.3 (F), f = 10Hz, a/IdAP = 1.2 (G), f = 1Hz, a/IdAP = 1.1 (H). For all examples, the initial input triggers the replay at oscillation phase ϕ = 0 (vertical dashed lines).

Figure 7: Phase invariance of replay speed. A: Dependence of the inter-assembly interval τk on interval index k for eight different oscillation phases at replay onset (legend) of the reference melody at baseline speed (Fig. 2A). B,C: Variability of replay timing, quantified by the coefficient of variation CVϕ(τk), of the first (k = 1; B) and the last inter-assembly interval (k = 7; C) as a function of oscillation frequency f and relative amplitude a/IdAP with offset Ī = 0 pA. Hatched areas represent parameter regions where stable replay occurs for fewer than four of the eight phases.

Figure 8: Stabilization of sequence replay by oscillatory input in the presence of noise. A,D: Dependence of the mean inter-assembly interval ⟨τk⟩k (top panels) and the coefficient of variation CVk(τk) of the inter-assembly intervals τk (bottom panels) on the amplitude σ of a correlated background noise (c = 0.9). Mean inter-assembly intervals and variation coefficients are obtained from the last four elements (k ∈ [4, 7]) of the sequence. Crosses and circles represent data for constant and oscillatory background input, respectively (legend). B–F: Background current (top) and spiking activity (bottom) for different configurations of background input: f = 59Hz, a/IdAP = 2.7 (B), Ī/IdAP = 0.885 (C), f = 25Hz, a/IdAP = 1 (E), Ī/IdAP = 0.305 (F) with ϕ = 0 and Ī = 0 pA. Examples are shown at noise amplitudes σ corresponding to the highest noise levels at which replay remains stable for both constant and oscillatory background input (see vertical dashed lines in A,D). Top and bottom rows correspond to parameter combinations resulting in a faster and a slower replay, respectively (⟨τk⟩k = 17ms and 40ms for σ = 0mV). Data shown here are obtained from a single noise realization.
